Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I was going to study, but then I'd just kind of drift through the day without actually getting much done. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open while listening to podcasts or music, thinking I was being productive. My 30 hours a week sounds like a lot, but a good chunk of that was probably just me sitting there, not really focused.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly panic when a deadline was close and try to cram everything in. It wasn't intentional—I just couldn't seem to get myself to stick to a schedule. When I didn't have immediate pressure, I'd convince myself I had more time than I actually did.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due soon or seeing my peers talking about an assignment in our group chat. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just scrolling through without really engaging. The anxiety of falling behind would push me to at least open it, even if I didn't do much once I was there.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a week or two where I was logging in daily, mostly because the peer support group I was connected with kept checking in on me. But that didn't last. Once the pressure eased up or I got overwhelmed with other stuff, I'd slip back into my old habits of avoiding it until the last minute.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd play them while doing other things, and somehow the information would stick better than reading. The discussion boards were okay too because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. That made me feel less alone in struggling.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just felt like too much effort, and I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but never did. The practice quizzes too—I knew they'd help, but the thought of seeing how much I didn't know made my anxiety spike, so I avoided them.
Q7: If something felt like it would directly help me pass or if it was quick and easy, I'd do it. Anything that seemed like it would take too long or require too much mental energy, I'd put off. I also engaged more when peers were doing the same activity—there's something about not wanting to be the only one who didn't participate.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't going to affect my grade directly, my brain just filed it under 'not important right now.' Required activities at least had consequences, so even if I dreaded them, I'd eventually force myself to attempt them. Optional activities felt like extra weight I couldn't carry.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while walking around or lying in bed. I also had a study group chat where we'd share notes and explain things to each other through voice messages. A lot of my actual learning happened through those conversations, not through clicking around on the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours talking through concepts with friends over Discord, working through problems together verbally. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. Or I'd replay a downloaded video multiple times until something clicked—the LMS just saw one view, but I probably watched certain parts five or six times.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video, then work with it completely offline for days. It was easier to study without the distraction of being connected to the internet. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have spent hours with that same material over the following week.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. I was already anxious, and then assignments started piling up from multiple courses. I felt paralyzed—like I had so much to do that I couldn't start anything. That's when my attendance really dropped. I'd skip live sessions because I was too stressed about being behind.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. Even just seeing the list of incomplete tasks would make my chest tight. I'd have competing responsibilities—family stuff, trying to maintain some social life—and the course always felt like it could wait, even when it couldn't.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. My body was there, but mentally I was checked out. The anxiety made it hard to actually absorb anything.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The bigger the task, the more I'd avoid it. It's like my brain would rather deal with the future consequences than face the present discomfort of actually doing the work. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure, but really I was just scared of failing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd float indefinitely. But they also stressed me out so much that I'd sometimes miss them anyway because I'd freeze up. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the 24 hours before anything was due, then went dead again afterward.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback—even constructive stuff—would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid that course for days. My resilience isn't great, so I'd take things personally even when I knew I shouldn't.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but was actually terrible for someone like me. I need external pressure to function. When things were self-paced or spread out, I'd procrastinate endlessly. The weeks with clear, structured deadlines were actually my most productive, even though I complained about them.
Q19: Having 20 courses to juggle made everything harder—I couldn't give proper attention to any single one. The peer support intervention actually helped a bit because it gave me some accountability. But the sheer volume of content was overwhelming. Shorter, more focused modules would have been easier to digest without triggering my anxiety.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd be on the LMS a lot, watching videos, reading discussions, but then when it came to actually submitting work, I'd freeze. It's not that I wasn't trying to engage—I was just too anxious or overwhelmed to produce anything. High activity doesn't mean high productivity when you're struggling mentally.
Q21: I can see that happening if someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material. For me, it was different—I was active but still struggled. But I have friends who barely touched the LMS, used textbooks and outside resources, and did fine. The system can't capture what happens outside its walls.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked things and for how long, but they can't show whether you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline studying, the peer conversations, the mental health struggles that affect engagement. A log might say I watched a video, but it can't tell you I was dissociating the whole time.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks mechanically. For me, real engagement happened in those moments when I was curious or when discussing ideas with peers—not when I was anxiously clicking through things to meet requirements.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety controlled my behavior, or how the peer support actually kept me from dropping out. It wouldn't show the voice messages with classmates that helped concepts click, or the times I cried from stress before forcing myself to submit something. The human struggle behind the numbers is completely invisible.
